sentences.text: I was talking about-- oh yes.
I was sent to Dicke Bertha.
She-- you-- you know, in the First World War, there was this big caliber cannon, which the Germans made to break the siege of some sort of front on the Western Front.
And they called this big caliber cannon Dicke Bertha.
And this woman was called the Dicke Bertha because she was very voluminous.
And she was the nurse in the Gypsy camp where the experiments were done on women prisoners and on twins.
Twins were kept in the block there.
So it was another hospital, but it was the hospital in the Gypsy camp.
And she dispensed the medicines.
So I went there, saluted, said what my business was.
And she sort of said something-- what the hell does he want that for?
But you know, it was a prescription signed by Mengele.
She issued it.
And as she was giving it to me, she sort of made like this, [GERMAN], you know, see to it that you disappear.
And I-- of course, I turned smartly, and I ran out.
And Mengele wanted his medicine back next day.
So they negotiated some more.
But in fact, the medicine was left.
But Mengele was sort of laughing that he'd won his bet.
You know, that she didn't like me, [LAUGHTER] this bloody woman.
So these are the sort of errands that I-- that I had, some more adventurous than others.
But they were all [? hum drum. ?]
All I knew was I knew about this Dicke Bertha by reputation.
And the reputation was quite terrifying.
I-- I thought of myself as if going, you know that story of Hansel and Gretel, when they are caught by the-- by the witch, and they are fed until they are fat, and then they are going to be killed.
And eventually, they manage to kill the witch.
Well, I felt like going to this witch, you know, that I was going to be somehow-- somehow-- for it.
But in fact, nothing happened to me.
But I walked into this thing.
And I walked up a corridor, went into her office.
She took me to the dispensary, gave me the stuff.
And she told me to disappear, and I disappeared.
So I knew-- I knew nothing and saw nothing of-- I saw the building.
Oh, everybody talked about it.
There was a sort of scuttlebutt that was going on.
The doctors talked about it.
Well, look.
The September transport was liquidated in about-- it's now '44, March.
I must tell you a little bit about the layout of the camp.
The railway yards was a space about half a mile long and perhaps 3-- or 400 yards across with perhaps three or four sidings.
No more than that.
It wasn't like a big-- big railway yard.
As you came into the camp through this gate, which says, Arbeit Macht Freude-- you know, Work Makes-- freude means-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] no, it's not gaiety.
It's-- it pleases you.
You know, Work Is Pleasing.
So as you go through the gate, the women's camps are on the left.
That's Birkenau A.
B-- B-- Auschwitz was-- was A.
Birkenau was B.
B eins, or BI, roman one, was the women's camp.
To the right was the men's camps.
And that was B zwei, BII.
And then there was a road, and all the gates to the B zweilagers were on the road away from the railway yard.
Not at the railway yard, but you had to walk practically a kilometer past the camp.
There was a-- well, let me explain to you about BII-- the BII section of the camp.
The BII section started with A.
So that was BIIa.
BIIa was not 30 yards.
It was half.
It was only the road and one side of huts.
So that was 15 huts.
But the length was the same.
And the-- the Germans used that as a sort of transit and reception camp, this A camp.
There was a road going from the railway yard to the main road, going-- if you're looking through the gate, you're looking north, OK?
So turning east, you're walking past the BII section.
Then you turn left, and you-- you're looking to the right again.
And you have a road, which was a service road for the SS personnel.
All the guards' duties were done from this road.
And all the towers were serviced from this road, not from the railway yard road.
There was a road there.
But that went straight to the crematoria and to the sauna.
To the north, there were these four factories, four chimneys.
You could only see chimneys from-- I'd never been to the crematoria during all this time that I was going from camp to camp.
I'd never actually been in the compounds of the crematoria, luckily.
Well, because I could see them.
You know, because when I was in Canada, one of the Canada sections was right next to crematoria number three.
Three-- that got blown up in September 1944.
I'll tell you about that later.
And just where the service road started, where the SS personnel had their barracks at each camp.
So the first section was A, BIIa.
BIIb-- that was our section, the family camp.
BIIb was 10,000 Jews from Czechoslovakia who were there for six months, and then they were liquidated.
Then there was BIIc, the next section.
Then there was another road, which went connecting between the service road of the SS to the railway.
So there were three camps, and then there was a road.
And then there was BIId.
That was the Mannerlager, where we, the Birkenau boys, were transferred to, from the family camp to the Mannerlager.
That was BIId.
E was the Gypsy camp.
And F was Canada.
That was already not a camp.
That was a store.
And then F.
And then there were-- A, B, C, D, E, F-- G were the crematoria compounds.
And one of the compounds, there were-- there were one, two, three, four, five, six-- seven separate compounds.
One was crematoria number one, which was disused.
That was a crematorium which, we were told-- I was told later-- you know, prisoners told us that that was the experimental crematorium, which was built to start with.
And then, on the experience of that, they built four others.
When they built the four others, one was never used.
So the first one was number two.
The second one was number three.
It was counted from this end, for some reason.
So it was two, three, four, five, and-- and one.
So it was one, two, three-- I don't know why they changed the numbering.
But that was three on the eastern side.
The sauna was in the middle.
So in March, you know, it was a punishment transport, I told you, the September transport.
All of the September transport went-- men, women, children, everyone who was still alive was taken to the A lager, out of the family camp.
And they were gassed.
And the men were gassed last.
When I got to the Mannerlager, I became friends with the Sonderkommando people.
And they told us how this was done.
You know, you took the men, like-- Freddy Hirsch committed suicide in the A-- in the A lager.
I hadn't seen his body.
And it-- it was just rumored.
But the cyanide pill was somehow smuggled through from-- from KB, from our block.
One of the doctors somehow organized it.
So that was the first part of the liquidation of the family camp, was the disappearance of-- of the-- of the Straffentransport.
And that's the transport that I would have been in, except that Leo took me out at the railway yard in Theresienstadt.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
So I am here, and Leo isn't.
So he swapped his life for mine.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When the men were being taken to the crematorium, first of all, they were kept in the A camp for a number of days.
Only the September.
That was the Straffentransport.

And it was the Straffentransport where-- where men who were usually taken to working camps, in this case, they were not taken because there was some sort of punishment, which the Germans administered.
What the punishment was, I doubt if it was for Heydrich.
Couldn't have been.
That was just a label.
But I think it was a punishment because these were the men who refused to continue getting the transports ready in Theresienstadt.
So they were the people who passively resisted.
If you passively resist, we'll get rid of you.
But how do you get rid of men who are-- you-- I mean, you know, you don't tell people you're going to gas them.
And they already knew that there were gas chambers.
And Freddy must've known, because he committed suicide.
And there's all kinds of talk that they were planning a revolt.
And Freddy was supposed to be the leader of the revolt.
And he didn't do it because he was a coward.

It's the sort of story that was created in Czechoslovakia under the communist regime to discredit Zionist leadership and Zionist youth movements because the policy of the communists became anti-Zionist.
It-- it's very invidious how prevalent the opinions are anti-Freddy amongst Czech Jews who have been subjected for 50 years to this communist propaganda.
Even today, you know, people sort of hear what was being said in the '50s.
Propaganda is a very-- very powerful thing.
Anyway, [? where ?] [? we are, ?] that's-- oh, I'm digressing.
These men were loaded onto lorries.
And they were told that they were going to an Arbeitslager.
And they went on a journey all night.
And they arrived in the morning back in Auschwitz.
And they were told they were somewhere in Poland and going to a sauna and schnell, schnell.
And now they were without sleep all night.
And they were sort of conditioned by being told over several days how they were going to this Arbeitslager, you see?
And then they were being driven all around Poland.
Now, I was told this by the-- by the Sonderkommando, who worked in the crematoria, by a friend of mine called Galen.
I'll tell you about Galen in a little while.
But I'm not yet in the Mannerlager, you see?
And then, there was a selection for men and a selection for women, and they departed.
And then there were only children and old people left.
So it was obvious what was going to happen.
I mean, even to me, it was obvious what was going to happen.
But remarkably enough, all the doctors and all the nurses who were in KB-- there were now two blocks, number 28 and 30, who were-- there was one woman's block, hospital, and a men's block.
All the nurses and all the-- the whole [GERMAN], the sanitary detail, who were all September transport people, were saved by Mengele.
He-- they-- they were the only people that didn't go.
Amongst them was a boy who is in this book that--
--I can't--
[GERMAN].
He is a boy whose father was in the Maennerlager.
And you know, you could, since it's a prison, you can do all kinds of things in prison.
It was somehow organized that he-- he stayed in the [GERMAN].
And a-- a lorry broke down, and some people weren't gassed.
Oh, well, I was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had plenty of free time, actually.
I wasn't busy.
And the [GERMAN] certainly didn't want me to do any work.
And he didn't see the joke of sending me around.
He's still alive.
I've met him, and he lives in Montreal.
I've met him.
So in 1992, I was in Canada.
Yes.
When I was doing this business with dysentery patients, I was called in to Dr.
Heller.
I knew I was in trouble.
And he says, I hear you're trading white bread.
So I said, no, you know.
I just started denying it.
So he says, come on, come clean, Misha.
You're trading with dysentery patients.
Why are you doing it?
So I says-- so I said, well, I'm hungry.
But I've seen you run around.
And you come several times.
Why do you need a loaf of bread?
So I said, well, I give half to my brother, Milush.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So he says, look, you'll kill your brother because you'll both catch dysentery from this bread.
I forbid you to do it.
But I'll arrange with the [GERMAN] that you are left with the leavings of this barrel of porridge.
And you call your brother to come, and you can scrape out the barrel, the two of you.
Now we had a sort of big tin can out of this porridge.
And Milush-- I sort of had to fetch Milush because he wasn't allowed to go.
So I had to fetch him, you know, as if-- as if he was going to [GERMAN].
All he had to do is bring his spoon.
And I'd have bits of the porridge, and then he'd-- he'd have the rest.
You couldn't actually satisfy Milush's hunger.
It didn't matter how much porridge was left to us.
And they left to me quite a generous portion.
And I would leave Milush, and I'd go inside because there was white bread being given out.
And I had to get my ration of white bread.
And I was sleeping with Milush in his block.
So in his block, I would go there when soup was ladled out so that I got my soup in the block where I was sleeping.
And I gave that soup to Milush.
So he had two bowls of soup, and he had my black bread in block 17, where he-- where he and I slept.
And he-- he-- and then he had the porridge.
And he was still ravenously hungry.
And I come out one-- one evening, and I see him still at this barrel, scraping at this black stuff.
And by this time, I was stronger than he because I was sort of well fed.
And he-- he was on this regime of working.
And-- and because he was growing, he became that emaciated and thin.
So I knocked the spoon out of his hand.
And I said, look, you are poisoning yourself with this black rind of the barrel.
It's never cleaned.
Well, they did clean it with hot water, but it was rotten-- rotten stuff from the previous day.
So next day, Milush wouldn't come.
He was too proud to come.
Now, hungry as he was, and we were brothers, you see, I had to beg his forgiveness in order for him to come and scrape out the barrel.
And I-- I felt so bad, you know, that I talked roughly with him when he was really so terribly, terribly hungry.
And he was sort of growing thinner every day.
Anyway, the liquidation came.
And they went off to [GERMAN], Leo and Milush together.
That's a work-- working camp where they both died.
They died perhaps a week before freedom because someone saw them leaving with the hospital in [GERMAN].
And the Germans probably did them in.
Yes, and as a matter of fact, this Heller, this Dr.
Heller was very kind to me, very sort of-- another completely altruistic.
I mean, he had his worries, and he had his family to look after.
And he had his work to do.
And you know, it must have been terrible for doctors not to be able to practice proper medicine because the Germans wouldn't furnish any medicine, nor bandages or anything.
They-- everything had to be improvised.
But he says, some boys are going to go to the [?
Mannerlager. ?] This is after the September transport had gone.
And I knew he was a September man.
And they were September-- the [GERMAN] stayed, you see?
So he says, we've talked to Mengele.
And they'll take some boys out.
And you are going to be amongst them.
Don't worry about it.
And he says, tell your brother that you're not going to die.
So he knew my brother.
But my brother wouldn't come and work for him.
My brother Leo.
So I say goodbye to Leo and Milush.
And I tell him what Dr.
Heller said.
And I-- I remember Milush-- Leo's look.
He looked at me, sort of, you're lying to me, you know, to make me feel good.
I could see he didn't believe me.
And I said, no, really, they told me that there are going to be some boys that are going to be selected to go to the Maennerlager.
And I'm going to be amongst them.
Now, this was like two or three weeks before the event.
And I had almost forgotten about it because by this time, it was like a morgue, you know?
We all knew that the old people and the children are going to die.
And there were no young people left.
There were only-- only the old and infirm and so on.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] And then came the selection.
And we all had to undress and parade in front of Mengele.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
And he comes to me.
And he starts shouting his head off, telling me off, because I have stripped like the other boys.
And he says, how am I supposed to recognize you amongst this [GERMAN], this, you know, multitude?
But he recognized me, although I wasn't in my clothes.
And now, I didn't know that-- did he wave me to the correct side or to the wrong side?
I had no idea.
But we all got dressed.
Did you still have your special--
Yes, yes.
Yes.
And all our numbers were taken down by the SS now, not by the-- not by the Jewish schreibers.
The people who were the administration were called schreibers.
Schreiber means write-- writer or accountant or office boy or whatever.
But they also had a-- that was a job, to be a schreiber because you wrote in the book of the day.
But this time, it was the SS that wrote our numbers.
And we all got dressed.
And I remember Helmut going up.
Helmut Spritzer.
He was the [GERMAN] at the gate-- actually, the [GERMAN] of the Lageralteste.
He wasn't the [GERMAN] at the gate in the Familienlager.
In the Familienlager, he was [GERMAN] of the Lageralteste.
And he asked for this other boy, who was crying and wanted to live.
And I think that was Harry Lowett.
We are now working out that we think it was Harry Lowett.
But Helmut was-- I remember thinking that I wasn't courageous enough to go up to Mengele and say, look, do I have to strip?
I just stripped.
Why do you think you were chosen by him?
Well, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] why-- why were 100 boys taken out?
Why did he save the sanitary unit?
He actually did save them.
It's a theory.
I think he was preparing the ground.
Don't forget, he-- he was a very intelligent man.
Well, for the end of the war.
It was summer of 1944.
The Russians were on the other side of the Vistula in Warsaw, which is a very short distance away from Auschwitz.
But the Germans must have seen the writing on the wall.
And I think that they did it-- it would look good in front of a judge.
